I don't think extensions are required (in rfc). If there's data following the compression methods then it must be extensions. So I recommend something like this instead:
```
if self.data[pointer:]: 
    self.extensions = parse_extensions(self.data[pointer:])
```

or (better) check for a minimal length of at least 1 extension:
```
if len(self.data[pointer:]) >= ... :
    self.extensions = parse_extensions(self.data[pointer:])
```

Collections.Iterable is not a sure fire way to check that objects will iterate safely in this use case, for example byte objects:

```python
In [27]: example = bytes([50,60,100,255,200])

In [28]: isinstance(example,collections.Iterable)
Out[28]: True

In [29]: isinstance(example,str)
Out[29]: False

In [30]: isinstance(example,collections.Iterable) and not isinstance(example,str)
Out[30]: True

In [31]: example
Out[31]: b'2<d\xff\xc8'

In [32]: for x in example:
    ...:     print(x)
    ...:
50
60
100
255
200
```
I think it would be better to use a set list of built in iterable container types, such as
```python
elif isinstance(prefix, (tuple, list, dict, set)):
# ...
```
But I do support this concept overall

I'm thinking that we should be really intentional/careful about this lifecycle.  Something about starting the next plugin mid-loop stands out to me as potentially problematic.

Consider this.

- `A` broadcasts event `x`
- Event `x` triggers `B` to start, broadcasting the `started` event.
- The `started` event triggers `C` to start.
- Finally `x` resumes being proadcast to the remaining plugins.

`parse_block_identifier` has logic to handle negative block numbers in a similar way Python does it for lists, e.g. `parse_block_identifier(-2)` will return the block before the last one. 
Currently this method would call getBlock('latest') for every invocation of a contract. What this pull request does is eliminates this extra call when block identifier (for the contract function call) is not numeric, e.g. 'latest'.
In my opinion, handling negative block identifiers for contract function calls should be removed for 2 reasons: (1) as @pipermerriam said it's unexpected behavior, (2) it's inconsistent with `web3.getBlock()` which doesn't handle negative block numbers. The problem with removing this logic is it will break backward compatibility.

This will not work as expected. By the time the method gets called, the name `engine` will reference the last item in the `engines` tuple. In other words, `engine` will be `'postgres_engine'` for all tests generated by this `for` loop.

One way to work around this is to use a function to create the test method. Something like this:
```py
def make_test(engine):
    def test(self):
        with getattr(self, engine)...
            ...
    return test
```
Then in the loop you can do `test = make_test(engine)`. [Explanation](https://eev.ee/blog/2011/04/24/gotcha-python-scoping-closures/).

Ran into a bit of an odd issue when playing around with pytype-all and this branch of importlab. The symptom is the following buggy behavior:
```
$ cat foo.py 
import collections
$ pytype-all foo.py
CRITICAL Cannot parse input files:
[Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/usr/local/google/home/rechen/collections.py'
```
What I think is happening is the following:
1. pytype-all adds a PytdFileSystem (`pytd_fs`) with its root at pytype/pytd/stdlib/2and3/.
2. Resolver.resolve_import calls `Resolver._find_file(pytd_fs, 'collections')` and finds pytype/pytd/stdlib/2and3/collections.pytd, so it resolves the file to `Local('collections.py', 'collections', pytd_fs)`.
3. This line converts 'collections.py' to '{cwd}/collections.py'.

`preparse()` is now called by `_complete_statement()`. It is asymmetric that there are no `self._preparsing_hooks`. Here's why.

If it's a multiline command, `preparse()` may not get all the user input. `_complete_statement()` really does two things: a) parse the user input, and b) accept more input in case it's a multiline command the passed string doesn't have a terminator. If we call `preparse()` at the beginning (which is now what it does), we call `preparse()` before we parse the input, but if it's a multiline command and there is no terminator, `preparse()` won't have all the input because it hasn't been entered yet. That's undesirable and misleading.

If we call `preparse()` after we are sure we have all the input, then by definition it can't be before we have parsed the input, because we have to parse it to know if we have it all. It's also misleading to have a hook called `preparse()` which doesn't get called until after everything is parsed.

The good news is that parsing (finally) is fast. If a developer wants to change the user input, they can write a post parsing hook, which gets passed the complete user input, modify it as desired, and reparse it.

By not having a `self._preparsing_hooks` we just make it less confusing. Postparsing hooks have all the input, and can choose to modify it if necessary before the statement is executed.

`round` returns a float that approximates an int. Depending on the representation, the `int` cast may do what you expect but may also round down to the integer below if the float repr is slightly less than the int, right? numPotential = int(columnInputs.size * self._potentialPct + 0.5)

I agree that just writing a line is nicer than formatting, but the double linebreak was actually deliberate to ensure that if we have additional output, we have the same spacing at the end of the hook's full output regardless of whether `stdout` or `stderr` exist:

```
eslint......................................................................................Failed
hookid: hook

Files were modified by this hook. Additional output:

Modified foo.py!

```

and for no `stdout` or `stderr`:

```
eslint......................................................................................Failed
hookid: hook

Files were modified by this hook.

```

That's unfortunately why the code is a little weird, since without the newline stuff we'd have two linebreaks at the end of the second case (since the final `write('\n')` is not surrounded by a check for `stdout` or `stderr`). Let me know if I'm missing a better way to avoid that.

I think between the pros and cons, it's kind of a wash. Either the `FoldScopeLocation` has to have a method it claims to implement (by complying with `BaseLocation`'s requirements) but always raises an error, or `Location` is the only type that has this method and calling it on another kind of location causes an `AttributeError`. Neither of these is a great option, but I think defining methods that aren't actually implemented is probably worse -- readers of `BaseLocation` would incorrectly assume that all locations implement `revisit()` when that isn't really true.

So unless you feel strongly about this, I'd leave it as-is for now.

I'm not sure what you mean by the order of commits.
You actually have more chances of getting into a conflict by doing a rebase, as you need to have no conflict with none of the commits since the two branches have diverged, while a merge will only consider the diff between the two branches. Thus, if someone has modified a file in a commit and then reverted the changes on that file in a later commit, you'd have more chances of getting conflicts using a rebase than a merge.
This is a very unlikely scenario.

The only advantage of rebase versus merge when having conflicts is that it might be easier to tackle a large number of conflict in small batches rather than the whole batch of conflicts at once.

Using `printf` in C++ is going to be ugly no matter what you do. `%d` means `int`, so anything else is wrong... but won't necessarily give an error if the argument is passed in a register. `%ld` and `%lld%` mean `[long] long int`, but that works wrong on Windows. Similarly with `%u`, `%lu`, and `%llu` for `unsigned`. And all of these are wrong for `size_t` or `ptrdiff_t`, which is really what you're trying to print here.
I would suggest either leaving the warning as-is, or casting the argument with `static_cast<long long>` and using `%lld`, using http://stackoverflow.com/a/6400404 if you want to write ultra-correct C code, or use `std::stringstream` with `operator<<` if you want a C++ solution.

If you create/export a datasource in Tableau Desktop, the resulting .tds looks like:

```
<datasource>
  ....
  <column> ....</column>
  <column> ....</column>
  <folder .../>
  <folder .../>
  ...
</datasource>
```

The <folder> tags are always below the <column> tags, despite both being children of datasource. For the XML parser, this doesn't necessarily make a difference. But for (1) parity with the way Tableau Desktop creates TDS files and (2) better readability for humans, I've decided to emulate this behaviour. To this end, I look for the index of the last <column> tag and insert the folder after it.

@zheng-da Consider any network, such as VGG, ResNet, etc. For any subgraph that is extracted by the TensorRT pass, the weights need to be provided to TensorRT at TensorRT engine construction time. These weights then become "baked in" the engine. Once the subgraph is substituted by a TensorRT node, these graph inputs become part of the TensorRT engine and are no longer used by the NNVM graph explicitly. Hence, they need to be removed, in order not to waste memory, and to prevent the confusion where some inputs still exists in the NNVM graph, but are not used anymore.
